meta.title: Expression of cowpea mosaic virus coat protein precursor in transgenic tobacco plants

abstractHtml:
Tobacco, Nicotiana tabacum L., supports cowpea mosaic virus (CPMV) replication and cell-to-cell movement, and thus may serve as a model system to study coat protein-mediated protection against CPMV. A chimeric gene consisting of the cauliflower mosaic virus 35S promoter, CPMV 60K coat proteins-precursor (CP-P) coding region, and the nopaline synthase polyadenylation signal was transferred to tobacco cv. Burley 21 via the Agrobacterium tumefaciens binary vector system. Gene integration and expression in the transgenic tobacco plants were confirmed by Southern and RNA dot blot analyses. Accumulation of CPMV 60K CP-P in transgenic plants, up to 2 µg/g of wet weight tissue, was detected by ELISA and Western blots. The results of Western blots and immunosorbent electron microscopy further indicated that CPMV CP-P neither undergoes auto-proteolysis to generate the mature viral coat proteins nor assembles into virus-like capsids, suggesting that processing of the CP-P may be required for virus assembly. Because CPMV neither induces symptoms in tobacco nor moves systemically, evaluation of the reactions of the transgenic plants to virus inoculation was based on virus accumulation in the inoculated leaves. Results from such infectivity experiments did not differentiate between CP-P expressers and vector-transformed plants. The transgenic tobacco plants expressing CP-P should provide valuable material for investigating comovirus polyprotein processing and capsid assembly in vivo.

† Present address: Monsanto Agricultural Company, New Products Division, 700 Chesterfield Village Parkway, Chesterfield, Missouri 63198, U.S.A.
summaryHtml:
Researchers created transgenic tobacco plants expressing the cowpea mosaic virus (CPMV) 60K coat protein precursor (CP-P) to investigate potential viral protection and polyprotein processing. Using Agrobacterium-mediated transformation, they inserted a chimeric gene containing the CPMV 60K CP-P coding region under control of the cauliflower mosaic virus 35S promoter into tobacco cv. Burley 21. Southern blot and RNA dot blot analyses confirmed gene integration and expression in transgenic plants. Western blots and ELISA detected accumulation of CPMV 60K CP-P at levels of 0.5 to 2.0 µg/g wet weight tissue (0.005-0.02% of total soluble proteins). However, immunoelectron microscopy and Western blot analysis revealed that the CP-P neither underwent autoproteolysis to generate mature viral coat proteins nor assembled into virus-like capsids, suggesting that processing may be required for capsid assembly. When transgenic plants were challenged with purified CPMV, no significant differences in virus replication were observed between CP-P expressers and vector-transformed controls, indicating that constitutive expression of the precursor did not confer protection against infection. The transgenic plants provide a valuable system for studying comovirus polyprotein processing and assembly mechanisms in plant tissue.

Key findings

  • Transgenic tobacco plants successfully expressed CPMV 60K coat protein precursor at levels of 0.5-2.0 µg/g wet weight tissue, confirmed by Southern blots, RNA dot blots, Western blots, and ELISA
  • The expressed CP-P did not undergo autoproteolysis to mature coat proteins nor assemble into virus-like capsids, suggesting processing is required for proper assembly
  • CP-P expression did not confer protection against CPMV infection, as virus accumulation was similar in both CP-P expressers and vector-transformed control plants
  • Gene integration followed Mendelian inheritance patterns (3:1 ratio in progeny), indicating stable chromosomal integration
